two-sum: Try using extra memory to speed up lookup: map each value to its index as you scan.
For each index i, the complement you need is target minus nums[i]. Have you seen it already?
A hash map from number to index lets you test the complement in O(1) time per element.
reverse-string: Two pointers: one at the start, one at the end; swap and move inward.
You can do this in place with O(1) extra space if the string is mutable.
Stop when the left index meets or crosses the right index.
valid-parentheses: Track opening symbols in order; a closing symbol must match the most recent unmatched opener.
A stack naturally models last-opened-first-closed structure.
If the stack is empty when you need to pop, or non-empty at the end, the string is invalid.
best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock: For each day as a sell day, the best buy is the minimum price seen before that day.
One pass: keep running minimum price and best profit so far.
You only need one transaction, so compare every price against the min so far.
merge-two-sorted-lists: Compare heads of both lists repeatedly and append the smaller node.
A dummy head node simplifies appending without special cases.
When one list empties, attach the remainder of the other list.
missing-number: The sum 0..n is n(n+1)/2; subtract the sum of the array to find the missing value.
XOR every index and every value; duplicates cancel and the missing number remains.
Sorting or a boolean array works but uses more time or space.
contains-duplicate: A set records values you have seen; duplicate if insert fails.
Sorting adjacent equal elements also reveals duplicates.
Prefer O(n) time with a hash set when memory allows.
valid-palindrome: Filter to alphanumeric characters, then compare from both ends.
Compare case-insensitively after normalizing each character.
maximum-subarray: Kadane: extend a segment while its sum helps; reset when sum drops below zero.
At each position, best ending here is max(nums[i], prevSum + nums[i]).
Track global maximum across all ending positions.
climbing-stairs: Ways to step n equals ways to (n-1) plus ways to (n-2).
This is the Fibonacci sequence with base cases for n=1 and n=2.
Iterative DP uses O(1) space; matrix exponentiation is possible for huge n.
linked-list-cycle: Slow and fast pointers: if there is a cycle, fast will eventually meet slow.
If fast reaches null, there is no cycle.
Move fast two steps per iteration and slow one step.
invert-binary-tree: Swap left and right children at each node, then recurse (or use a queue).
Handle null children explicitly when swapping.
longest-substring-without-repeating-char: Sliding window: expand when the character is new, shrink from the left when it repeats.
A map stores last index of each character in the current window.
Update max length whenever the window is valid.
3sum: Sort first, then fix one index and use two pointers for the rest.
Skip duplicate values after sorting to avoid duplicate triplets.
Target for two-sum becomes zero minus the fixed element.
product-of-array-except-self: Prefix products of all elements to the left, suffix products to the right.
Answer at i is prefix[i-1] times suffix[i+1].
Can do O(1) extra space by output array as prefix storage, then multiply by suffix pass.
number-of-islands: DFS or BFS from each unvisited land cell; mark visited cells as water or use a visited set.
Boundary check: stay inside the grid.
course-schedule: Cycle in a directed graph means impossible schedule; topological sort detects it.
Compute indegree; repeatedly remove nodes with indegree zero.
If you process all n nodes, no cycle; otherwise a cycle exists.
coin-change: DP: minCoins(amount) = 1 + min over coins of minCoins(amount - coin).
Base case: amount zero needs zero coins; unreachable amounts stay infinity.
Bottom-up iteration avoids stack depth issues.
top-k-frequent-elements: Count frequencies with a hash map, then bucket by frequency or use a heap.
Bucket sort: index is frequency; list of numbers with that count.
Min-heap of size k on (frequency, value) pairs also works.
lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-binary-searc: If both values are smaller than root, LCA is in the left subtree.
If both are larger, LCA is in the right subtree.
Otherwise the current node splits them and is the LCA.
implement-trie-prefix-tree: Each node holds a map of char to child node and an end-of-word flag.
Insert walks or creates path; search follows path; prefix checks same path exists.
26 children or a map scales to any alphabet size.
container-with-most-water: Two pointers at both ends; area is width times min of the two heights.
Move the pointer with the smaller height inward to possibly increase area.
Greedy works because shorter side limits the area.
search-in-rotated-sorted-array: Binary search: at least one half is normally sorted.
Compare mid with target and endpoints to decide which half is safe.
Handle duplicates by shrinking bounds when nums[left] equals nums[mid].
group-anagrams: Sort characters of each string to get a canonical key, or count letters.
Hash map from key to list of strings.
Counting array of length 26 works for lowercase English letters.
rotate-image: Transpose the matrix, then reverse each row (or reverse layers in place).
Rotate four cells at a time in layers from outside to inside.
validate-binary-search-tree: Each node must lie within (min, max) bounds inherited from ancestors.
Inorder traversal of a valid BST yields strictly increasing values.
Pass allowed min and max down the recursion.
binary-tree-level-order-traversal: BFS with a queue; process level by level using level size.
Alternate: DFS with depth index appending to lists.
Track current level count before dequeuing children.
rotting-oranges: Multi-source BFS from all rotten cells at minute zero.
Each round spreads to fresh neighbors in four directions.
If fresh remain after BFS completes, return -1.
word-break: DP: canBreak[i] means s[0..i) is segmentable using dictionary words.
Try every word from dictionary as suffix ending at i.
Memoization on substring also works but may be slower.
task-scheduler: Count the most frequent task; it drives minimum idle slots between batches.
Arrange the max frequency block first, then fill gaps.
Formula uses counts and cooldown n; also consider greedy simulation.
trapping-rain-water: Two pointers from left and right; track max left and max right heights.
Water at a position is limited by the shorter side of the two maxes.
Move the pointer with the smaller max inward.
merge-k-sorted-lists: Min-heap of (value, list index, node) or divide-and-conquer pairwise merge.
Compare k heads at each step with a heap for O(n log k) time.
Repeated merge of two lists works but can be less efficient.
word-search-ii: Build a trie from words; DFS from each cell, prune when prefix not in trie.
Mark cells visited during DFS, backtrack after.
Remove words from trie when found to avoid duplicate work.
median-of-two-sorted-arrays: Binary search the partition in the shorter array so left halves have equal total length.
Ensure max of left halves is less than or equal to min of right halves.
Handle odd total length by taking max of left or min of right halves.
longest-valid-parentheses: Stack stores indices; push -1 as base for length calculation.
On ')', pop and compute length if stack non-empty, else push current index.
DP: dp[i] length ending at i also works.
minimum-window-substring: Sliding window with two pointers; expand until valid, shrink from left while valid.
Count needed characters vs current window counts.
Track best window when all required frequencies are met.
edit-distance: DP[i][j] = cost to convert first i chars of word1 to first j of word2.
Space can be reduced to two rows.
largest-rectangle-in-histogram: Monotonic stack of indices with increasing heights.
When a shorter bar appears, pop and compute area with popped height as minimum.
Append zero at end to flush the stack.
n-queens: Backtrack row by row; place queen if column and diagonals are free.
Track occupied columns and two diagonal directions.
Try each column in the current row and recurse.
maximum-profit-in-job-scheduling: Sort jobs by end time; binary search previous non-overlapping job.
DP[i] = max profit using first i jobs: skip or take job i.
Taking job i adds its profit to DP at the last compatible index.
serialize-and-deserialize-binary-tree: Preorder with null markers for missing children, joined by delimiter.
Or level-order BFS with queue and nulls for absent nodes.
Deserialize rebuilds tree using the same traversal order.
find-median-from-data-stream: Two heaps: max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half.
Keep sizes balanced so median is top of larger heap or average of both tops.
Insert pushes into appropriate heap then rebalances.
